channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: ICQIA Expands Global Reach With New Charter And Members
item.description:
Curator's Take: This expansion of the International Council of Quantum Industry Associations represents a crucial step in creating coordinated global governance for the rapidly maturing quantum industry. With UKQuantum and the Korea Quantum Industry Association joining the council, ICQIA now spans key quantum powerhouses across multiple continents, creating a more comprehensive framework for addressing international standards, trade policies, and collaborative research initiatives. This kind of cross-border coordination becomes increasingly vital as quantum technologies approach commercial viability and nations seek to balance competitive advantages with the need for interoperable systems and shared safety standards. The timing is particularly significant as governments worldwide are ramping up quantum investments and need unified industry voices to guide policy decisions that will shape the global quantum ecosystem.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The global quantum industry took a step toward greater international coordination as the International Council of Quantum Industry Associations (ICQIA) adopted a new charter and expanded its membership to welcome two additional member organisations: UKQuantum and the Korea Quantum Industry Association (KQIA).The additions expand the council’s geographic representation and reinforce […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Companies Join World Economic Forum’s Technology Pioneers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This recognition by the World Economic Forum signals that quantum technologies are transitioning from laboratory curiosities to commercially viable solutions that global leaders consider essential for future economic competitiveness. The inclusion of companies spanning photonic quantum computing, quantum networking, and quantum communications demonstrates the breadth of the quantum ecosystem's maturation across multiple technological approaches. This designation typically opens doors to influential partnerships and investment opportunities, potentially accelerating the commercialization timeline for these quantum technologies. The WEF's acknowledgment also suggests that quantum computing is increasingly viewed as a foundational technology for addressing global challenges in cybersecurity, financial modeling, and complex optimization problems.

Insider Brief The World Economic Forum has named several quantum technology companies to its 2026 Technology Pioneers cohort, a sign of the growing role of quantum innovation in the next generation of computing, communications and cybersecurity solutions. The selected firms span multiple segments of the quantum ecosystem, including photonic quantum computing, quantum networking, quantum communications […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Realizing the fermionic Laughlin state on a trapped-ion quantum processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant milestone in quantum simulation, as the fermionic Laughlin state is a highly exotic quantum many-body state that's notoriously difficult to create and study in traditional laboratory settings. The successful realization of this state on a trapped-ion quantum processor demonstrates the growing maturity of quantum hardware for tackling fundamental physics problems that are computationally intractable for classical computers. What makes this particularly exciting is that Laughlin states are connected to fractional quantum Hall effects and topological quantum computing concepts, suggesting that near-term quantum devices may soon be able to explore entirely new regimes of quantum matter. This collaboration between University of Washington researchers and Amazon's quantum team also showcases how cloud-based quantum processors are becoming powerful tools for cutting-edge scientific discovery.

This post was contributed by Lingnan Shen, Mao Lin, Cedric Yen-Yu Lin, Di Xiao, and Ting Cao Quantum computing is beginning to simulate forms of matter that are difficult to study in the lab or compute classically. In this work, researchers from University of Washington and Amazon Braket demonstrate one such milestone. They realized a […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum X Labs and IQCC Partner to Evaluate AI-Based Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction by combining Quantum X Labs' AI-driven error correction algorithms with Quantum Machines' proven quantum control infrastructure. The collaboration is particularly noteworthy because it moves beyond theoretical error correction research into real-world testing on actual quantum hardware, which is essential for validating whether AI-based approaches can outperform traditional error correction methods. As quantum computers scale up, the integration of machine learning techniques for error correction could prove crucial for achieving fault-tolerant quantum computing, making this evaluation a potential bellwether for the field's progress toward commercially viable quantum systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum X Labs Inc. (Nasdaq: QXL) (“Quantum X Labs” or the “Company”), an advanced quantum technologies company, and IQCC, a Quantum Machines company, today announced the signing of a strategic cooperation agreement, under which Quantum X Labs will evaluate its AI-based quantum error-correction technology on Quantum Machines’ quantum control infrastructure. The primary objective is to run Quantum X Labs’ proprietary AI-driven error correction algorithm in a fully integrated […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Bosonic Cyclic Codes: Trading Stabilizers for Gaussian Non-Clifford Phase Gates
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research addresses a fundamental tension in bosonic quantum error correction: while rotation-symmetric codes like cat and binomial codes excel at protecting quantum information from photon loss and dephasing, they severely limit the types of logical operations you can perform fault-tolerantly. The new bosonic cyclic codes offer an elegant solution by allowing researchers to deliberately sacrifice some error detection capability in exchange for a rich set of logical phase gates that can be implemented using relatively simple Gaussian operations. This represents a crucial step toward making bosonic codes practical for quantum computing, as previous approaches required complex non-linear operations to implement the full gate set needed for universal quantum computation. The ability to tune this protection-versus-controllability tradeoff could make bosonic platforms much more viable for near-term quantum algorithms where some error tolerance can be exchanged for computational versatility.

Bosonic codes offer hardware-efficient approaches to quantum error correction, with the best encodings offering effective protection of idle quantum information against loss and dephasing - particularly rotation-symmetric codes, which include the cat and binomial code families. However, rotation-symmetric codes are only naturally endowed with a single logical Pauli gate, while other logical gates require the use of non-linear operations, obstructing the utility of these codes for realizing quantum algorithms. Here, we balance error protection with controllability by introducing bosonic cyclic codes: a generalization of rotation-symmetric codes that enable the measured tradeoff of error protection properties for fault-tolerant logical phase gates. Through our general construction, we find that sacrificing the detectability of a single photon loss relative to a rotation-symmetric code can yield a number of logical phase gates commensurate with the original rotation symmetry order of the code, all achievable via passive Gaussian rotations. Giving the corresponding generalizations of cat and binomial codes - which we dub cyclic cat and Vandermonde codes, respectively - we further find that many of the desirable properties of these codes transfer to the bosonic cyclic code setting. We go on to discuss the larger $SU(2)$ symmetry and rotation gates of the codes, which yield additional stabilizers and logical Pauli gates, as well as new non-Clifford gates for the smallest `kitten' binomial code, and provide a new error detection protocol. Finally, we introduce a general paradigm for converting higher-order stabilizers to logical gates, as in our generalization of rotation-symmetric codes, and apply it to several multimode bosonic codes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Analog Quantum Asynchronous Event-Based Graph Neural Network
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a fascinating convergence of quantum computing and neuromorphic computing, proposing to implement event-based graph neural networks directly on neutral-atom quantum processors using Rydberg interactions. What makes this particularly compelling is how the authors cleverly map streaming event data from event cameras onto trapped atoms, where the physical positioning of atoms mirrors the spatio-temporal relationships in the data—essentially turning the quantum hardware into a native graph processing engine. The approach could unlock significant advantages for processing the sparse, high-speed data streams from event cameras used in robotics and autonomous systems, where traditional neural networks often struggle with computational efficiency. While still theoretical, this work opens an intriguing new direction for quantum machine learning that leverages the natural parallelism and continuous dynamics of analog quantum systems rather than forcing discrete quantum gate operations.

Asynchronous, event-based graph neural networks (AEGNNs) have recently emerged as an efficient paradigm for processing the sparse and high-temporal-resolution data from event cameras. In this paper, we propose quantum analog AEGNNs (QA-AEGNNs), a novel framework to implement an AEGNN on a neutral-atom quantum computer. Neutral-atom quantum processors offer a programmable analog quantum computing platform based on controllable Rydberg-atom interactions. To this end, we map the streaming event data to an array of trapped neutral atoms, where each atom represents a graph node (event) and is positioned such that geometric proximity reflects the spatio-temporal neighborhood of events. The native Rydberg Hamiltonian of the quantum processor is programmed to mirror the message-passing computations of the AEGNN, with atomic qubit states serving as node feature embeddings and inter-atom interactions realizing graph edges. Furthermore, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical training scheme in which the analog Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., laser pulse amplitudes and detunings) are optimized using classical feedback to learn the quantum AEGNN model from data. Our approach leverages the continuous Hamiltonian dynamics and massive parallelism of neutral-atom quantum systems to natively execute event-based graph computations with potential accuracy improvements

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nonreciprocal quantum rotation sensing via virtual-excitation enhancement in a spinning cavity
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating approach to quantum rotation sensing that exploits virtual excitations in ultrastrong light-matter coupling to achieve enhanced sensitivity. The key innovation lies in using the Sagnac effect within a spinning cavity to create direction-dependent coupling that encodes rotational information not just in the observable spectrum, but also in virtual excitations that normally can't be directly accessed. What makes this particularly exciting is the nonreciprocal nature of the sensing scheme, where the sensitivity depends on the direction of rotation, potentially enabling more precise measurements and opening new avenues for quantum gyroscopes. This work bridges fundamental quantum optics with practical sensing applications, suggesting that the exotic physics of virtual excitations could become a valuable resource for next-generation quantum sensors.

Quantum sensing with high precision and sensitivity plays an important role in quantum technologies and quantum information processing. Here, we propose a nonreciprocal quantum metrological scheme for estimating rotational angular velocity in a hybrid light-matter platform, where the setup consists of a spinning ring cavity coupled to a two-level system and an auxiliary bosonic mode. Through the Sagnac effect, the angular velocity is converted into a direction-dependent detuning, which modifies the effective light-matter dressing of the hybrid system. As a result, the angular velocity is encoded not only into the renormalized hybrid-mode spectrum, but also into the virtual excitations generated by ultrastrong coupling. These virtual excitations modify the polaritonic frequency response to rotation and enhance the quantum Fisher information (QFI) associated with angular velocity estimation, without requiring direct extraction of virtual excitations. Moreover, since the Sagnac-Fizeau shift enters the virtual-transition energy denominators, the metrological response becomes intrinsically different for opposite driving directions, leading to a tunable nonreciprocal sensitivity contrast. In addition, we also discuss a readout scheme and show that bundle emission coincidence counting can serve as an auxiliary direction-dependent readout channel. Our results provide a route toward exploiting nonreciprocal light-matter dressing and virtual excitations as resources for quantum rotation sensing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Inherent flux crosstalk and coupler-driven single-qubit gates in superconducting circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article reveals a fascinating example of turning a quantum computing bug into a feature by showing how flux crosstalk between superconducting qubits can actually enable new control mechanisms. The researchers discovered that time-varying magnetic fluxes create cross-voltage driving effects that allow single-qubit operations to be performed through the coupler elements themselves, rather than requiring dedicated microwave control lines for each qubit. This finding could significantly simplify the hardware architecture of large-scale quantum processors by reducing the number of control lines needed, which is currently a major engineering challenge as systems scale to hundreds or thousands of qubits. The work demonstrates how deeper understanding of unwanted interactions in quantum systems can sometimes lead to more efficient control strategies.

Crosstalk refers to unwanted qubit addressing. This is particularly detrimental when scaling up quantum information systems because unintended interactions limit their overall performance. For superconducting qubits, tunable couplings and frequency tunability achieved through externally applied magnetic fluxes enable high-fidelity entangling gates; however, they also introduce crosstalk through unintended flux coupling. In this work, we investigate the impact of time-dependent external magnetic fluxes in quantized circuits on superconducting qubit couplings. We find that non-trivial cross-voltage driving emerges between capacitively linked qubits when the magnetic flux threading the SQUID loop of a qubit varies in time, in a manner analogous to Faraday's law of induction. Crucially, we show that this effect enables fast single qubit control through the coupler element in standard tunable-coupler architectures, potentially eliminating the need for individual microwave $XY$ control lines.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM Announces Novel Quantum Error Correction Approach Toward Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement from IQM represents a potentially game-changing development in quantum error correction, as achieving three orders of magnitude improvement in logical error rates while using eight times fewer physical qubits would dramatically accelerate the timeline to fault-tolerant quantum computing. The surface code has long been considered the gold standard for quantum error correction due to its practical implementability, so any approach that significantly outperforms it deserves serious attention from the quantum community. If validated, this breakthrough could address one of the most pressing bottlenecks in quantum computing - the massive overhead required for error correction that currently makes large-scale quantum algorithms impractical. The combination of lower error rates and reduced qubit requirements could bring commercially viable fault-tolerant quantum computers years closer to reality.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — IQM Quantum Computers, the global leader in superconducting quantum computers, has developed a novel quantum error-correcting code that achieves up to three orders of magnitude lower logical error rates than the surface code, also requiring up to eight times fewer physical qubits. Unlike many alternative high-performance quantum error-correction approaches, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Schmidt Decomposition-Based Methods for Efficient Quantum Image Encoding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in quantum computing today: making quantum algorithms practical on current noisy hardware with limited resources. The authors demonstrate that Schmidt decomposition can dramatically reduce circuit complexity for quantum image processing, with their FRQI approach achieving a remarkable 97 percent reduction in circuit depth while maintaining excellent image quality. This work is particularly significant because it bridges the gap between theoretical quantum image processing techniques and the harsh realities of NISQ devices, where every gate counts and circuit depth directly impacts success rates. The trade-off analysis between computational efficiency and image fidelity provides a practical roadmap for implementing quantum image applications on near-term quantum computers, potentially accelerating the development of quantum machine learning and computer vision applications.

In quantum image processing, a fundamental step is encoding classical image data into quantum states. This can be achieved using methods such as Flexible Representation of Quantum Images (FRQI), Quantum Probability Image Encoding (QPIE), and Novel Enhanced Quantum Representation (NEQR). However, on real quantum hardware, these encodings can quickly lead to circuits with many gates, large circuit depth, and high qubit usage, which is a problem for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. In this work, we investigate whether low-rank state approximation, formulated via Schmidt decomposition, can help reduce this complexity. The method keeps only the most significant parts of a quantum state's entanglement structure, making state preparation more efficient while preserving most of the image information. We compare the three encoding techniques in their original form and with low-rank approximation, evaluating metrics such as circuit depth, CNOT count, MSE, and visual quality of reconstructed images. The results reveal meaningful trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency, with the FRQI model achieving a 97 percent reduction in circuit depth while maintaining a near-perfect reconstruction (MSE of about 0.27). This demonstrates the potential of low-rank techniques for advancing practical quantum image processing on near-term hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QTREX Awarded Government Grant to Advance Native RF Dielectric Material for Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked aspect of quantum computing development - the need for specialized materials that can operate in the extreme conditions required by quantum processors. QTREX's grant to develop native RF dielectric materials addresses a fundamental infrastructure challenge, as current quantum systems often rely on adapted conventional materials that weren't designed for the ultra-low noise, cryogenic environments where qubits operate. The Israel Innovation Authority's backing suggests growing government recognition that quantum computing breakthroughs depend not just on qubit designs and algorithms, but also on purpose-built supporting technologies like specialized substrates and packaging materials. This type of materials engineering work may seem mundane compared to qubit count announcements, but it's essential for scaling quantum computers from laboratory prototypes to practical systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QTREX Quantum Ltd. (Nasdaq: QTEX) (“QTREX” or the “Company”), a company focused on advancing Additively Manufactured Electronics (“AME”) for quantum computing infrastructure, today announced that the Israel Innovation Authority (“IIA”) has awarded the Company an approximately $1 million grant to support the development of a purpose-built dielectric material engineered for […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The fidelity of controlled quantum teleportation in a noisy environment
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in quantum networking: how to maintain the quality of quantum teleportation when real-world noise inevitably corrupts the entangled states that make it possible. The work is particularly valuable because it examines controlled quantum teleportation, where a third party must give permission for the process to complete, making it essential for secure quantum communication networks. The surprising discovery that fidelity doesn't always degrade monotonically with noise - sometimes actually improving before declining - could inform new strategies for optimizing quantum protocols in noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. These insights are crucial as quantum networks move from laboratory demonstrations toward practical deployment, where understanding and mitigating noise effects will determine whether quantum teleportation can reliably serve as the backbone of future quantum internet infrastructure.

In this work, we investigate controlled quantum teleportation in the presence of noisy channels acting on the three-qubit resource state. We employ a series of generalized noisy channels that bridge the dephasing channels and amplitude damping channels while encompassing extensive intermediate scenarios. We provide an in-depth analysis of the degradation of the maximal average fidelity and the optimal average fidelity in controlled quantum teleportation induced by such noisy channels by deriving the analytical expression and examining several special cases. The analytical expression shows that attaining the optimal average fidelity requires Charlie's cooperation in performing a measurement at suitably chosen angles, and is also related to the initial state and the channel parameters. Our analysis reveals that the optimal average fidelity does not always decrease monotonically with the evolution parameter, instead, it first decreases and then increases. This non-monotonic behavior depends on the entanglement of the initial resource state, as well as on the parameters of the channel traversed by the first qubit.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: ELECTRA AI and Naoris Quantum Protocol Partner on Quantum-Safe Battery Intelligence
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a crucial convergence of quantum-safe cryptography with the rapidly expanding Internet of Things battery ecosystem, addressing a real vulnerability as quantum computers threaten current encryption methods. As AI-powered battery management systems become ubiquitous in everything from electric vehicles to grid storage, securing these always-connected devices against future quantum attacks is becoming a practical necessity rather than a theoretical concern. The collaboration highlights how post-quantum cryptography is moving beyond academic research into commercial applications, particularly in critical infrastructure where compromised battery systems could have cascading effects on power grids and transportation networks. While still early-stage, this type of proactive quantum-safe integration across IoT devices signals the industry's growing recognition that quantum-resistant security needs to be built in from the ground up, not retrofitted later.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — ELECTRA AI (“Electra”) and Naoris Quantum Protocol Inc (“Naoris Quantum Protocol”) today announced a partnership to bring post-quantum cybersecurity to AI battery intelligence, the fast-growing layer where always-connected battery systems meet the AI models that monitor, optimize, and control them. The two companies are pairing Electra’s AI Brain for Batteries™ platform with Naoris […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM and Real Asset Acquisition Move Forward with Public Listing Plans
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development marks a significant milestone for the quantum computing industry as IQM, one of Europe's leading quantum hardware companies, moves closer to becoming a publicly traded entity through its SPAC merger with Real Asset Acquisition Corp. The public listing would provide IQM with enhanced capital access to scale their superconducting quantum computer technology and compete more effectively with established players like IBM and Google in the global quantum market. As one of the few pure-play quantum computing companies pursuing public markets, IQM's listing could serve as a bellwether for investor appetite in quantum technologies and potentially pave the way for other quantum startups to follow suit. This move comes at a critical time when quantum hardware companies need substantial funding to bridge the gap between current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and the fault-tolerant systems needed for commercial quantum advantage.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — IQM Finland Oy, a global leader in full-stack superconducting quantum computers (“IQM”, “IQM Quantum Computers” or the “Company”), and Real Asset Acquisition Corp. (Nasdaq: RAAQ), a special purpose acquisition company (“RAAQ”), announced today that the registration statement on Form F-4 (the “Registration Statement”), relating to their previously announced business combination, […]
